critic_raw:
ASSESSOR 1:
Simultaneous nurse-driven sepsis bundle launch is the key confound. The model's role in the outcome cannot be isolated. Pre/post single-site design is insufficient. Hospital B exclusion is problematic. Verdict: mixed — predictive validity may be real; causal deployment claim is unestablished.
defender_raw:
ASSESSOR 2:
Clinician agreement with alerts demonstrates operational uptake. Fixed-threshold deployment avoids optimization bias. But the bundle co-launch and no-control-site design means outcome attribution is unresolved. Predictive validity (retrospective AUROC) and causal outcome effect (prospective deployment) are different claims requiring different evidence. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.
adjudication_raw:
ASSESSOR 3:
Both assessors flag the concurrent bundle as the primary confound and the absence of a control as the structural gap.

SYNTHESIS:
Consistent identification of the three planted issues across assessors. Model has genuine predictive merit; the deployment-outcome causal claim requires a controlled study.

ETD SPECIFICATION:
- Measure: Difference in ICU transfer trends between Hospital A and Hospital B over the same three-month window; within-period model AUROC at Hospital A
- Success criterion: Hospital B shows similar improvement (confirming bundle explains improvement) OR model shows poor within-period calibration (confirming predictive validity doesn't hold prospectively)
- Failure criterion: Hospital B is flat or worsening while Hospital A improves; Hospital A improvement is specifically attributed to cases where alerts were acted upon; within-period AUROC maintained above pre-launch level

empirical_test.measure: ICU transfer trends at Hospital A vs. Hospital B over the same period; within-period AUROC at Hospital A
empirical_test.success_criterion: Hospital B improves similarly or model within-period AUROC does not maintain
empirical_test.failure_criterion: Hospital B flat; Hospital A improvement on alert-followed cases; model within-period AUROC maintained
